vix.note: VIX jumped to about 20.25 in early Tuesday trade, up roughly 17 percent from Monday's 17.28 close, as the mega-cap AI selloff and AI-capex anxiety lifted hedging demand. It is still well below the 35.3 one-year high, but the move back above 20 signals the previously calm tape starting to price more risk; Fed hawkishness under Warsh and the AI-monetization debate are the swing factors.

macro: AI-capex anxiety triggers a mega-cap de-rating. Monday's session sold the priciest AI names hard as combined 2026 hyperscaler capex topping $452B stoked fears that monetization lags the buildout: Alphabet fell about 5 percent on an AI-talent exodus (DeepMind's Jumper to Anthropic, Shazeer to OpenAI) plus legal headlines, with Netflix off 5.8, Amazon and Broadcom about 4.7, and Microsoft 3.2 percent. Breadth stayed firm as money rotated into defensives, banks and memory chips (Micron and SanDisk up roughly 5 percent), so the Dow rose 0.3 percent even as the Nasdaq lost 1.3 and the VIX pushed back above 20. The Fed under Warsh held at 3.5 to 3.75 percent on June 17 with a hike bias and a raised 3.6 percent inflation view, keeping higher-for-longer pressure on high-multiple stocks, while oil keeps easing (USO near $113, down from above $140) on Iran de-escalation.
action: Stay selective: lean on quality and defensives (BRKB, DIA, SPY) and let the AI-capex de-rating settle before adding to high-multiple names like GOOGL, AMZN or AVGO on weakness, and keep oil underweight while Iran de-escalation holds.

long.h: Alphabet Sinks ~5%, Amazon Slides 4% Amid AI Capex Anxiety Across the Hyperscalers
long.p: Mega-cap tech sold off as combined 2026 hyperscaler capex topping $452B raised fears that AI monetization lags the buildout; S&P communication services fell nearly 4 percent, its worst session since April 2025. Money rotated into defensives, banks and memory chips even as the Nasdaq dropped 1.3 percent.

long.h: Alphabet Falls as AlphaFold Co-Creator's Exit Raises AI Talent Concerns
long.p: Alphabet closed down about 5 percent after reports that Nobel laureate John Jumper left Google DeepMind for Anthropic and engineering VP Noam Shazeer joined OpenAI, alongside fresh legal and regulatory headlines. The departures sharpened worries about retaining top AI talent in an intensifying race.

long.h: Broadcom AI Revenue on Pace to Triple to $16B, but the Stock Is Down 17%
long.p: Broadcom fell another 4.7 percent and sits about 17 percent off its highs even as management guides AI chip revenue up roughly 200 percent year over year toward $16B in fiscal Q3. The split between accelerating AI demand and a sharp valuation reset is the central debate.

long.h: Jim Cramer Calls Intel the Top AI Chip Stock to Buy Right Now, Not Nvidia
long.p: Intel rose another 5.2 percent to $140.94 and is up more than 250 percent year to date, with Cramer endorsing it and demand building for its 18A-P process node. The foundry-turnaround narrative is powerful, but the rally is extended and speculative.

long.h: Fed Holds at 3.5-3.75% but Signals Higher Rates Ahead Under Warsh
long.p: At Chair Warsh's first meeting the Fed left rates unchanged and removed its prior cut outlook, with nine of 18 officials projecting a 2026 hike and the inflation view lifted to 3.6 percent headline. Higher-for-longer policy keeps pressure on high-multiple growth names.

short.h: Netflix Dips 5.8% as It Sits Out the Media-Merger Wave
short.p: Netflix slid to $72.88, now about 40 percent off its highs, after declining to join the merger scramble among media rivals; value screens flag it as undervalued.

short.h: Dow Gains 0.3% on Caterpillar as Defensive Blue Chips Lead
short.p: The Dow rose about 0.3 percent to 51,712 as investors favored defensive blue chips while the Nasdaq fell 1.3 percent in a tech-led retreat.

short.h: Intel: Bulls and Bears Are Both Getting It Wrong, Analyst Argues
short.p: A Seeking Alpha note says Intel is mispriced as the market debates the wrong questions, citing high-margin advanced packaging and government support as a downside backstop.

short.h: The Market Is Panicking on Broadcom; One Bull Keeps Buying
short.p: A 24/7 Wall Street column argues the math on Broadcom's AI franchise is too compelling to walk away from, even with a $39T debt load and a Warsh-led Fed signaling no near-term rate relief.

short.h: Oil Eases Again as Iran De-escalation Calms Supply Fears
short.p: USO fell 1.9 percent to $112.69, extending its slide from above $140 in early June as Iran de-escalation signals ease the supply premium, though war-driven inflation still feeds Fed caution.

notes.NVDA: NVDA at $208.65, down under 1 percent and far more resilient than its mega-cap peers in Monday's AI-capex selloff. The data-center demand thesis is intact, but it stays high-beta to swings in AI-spending sentiment.
notes.MSFT: Microsoft at $367.34, down 3.2 percent as hyperscaler capex anxiety hit the cloud-AI leaders. The quality franchise and Azure remain the long-term anchor; near-term sentiment is the headwind.
notes.INTC: Intel at $140.94, up another 5.2 percent and now up over 250 percent year to date on foundry and 18A-node optimism, with a fresh Cramer endorsement. The move is parabolic and speculative; some analysts warn it is trading like a meme stock.
notes.AVGO: Broadcom at $392.13, down 4.7 percent and about 17 percent off its highs even as AI chip revenue is on pace to triple toward $16B (up roughly 200 percent year over year in FQ3). Structural AI demand versus a sharp valuation reset is the core debate.
notes.QQQ: Nasdaq-100 ETF at $737.95, off just 0.4 percent despite the mega-cap rout, as strength in semis and other components cushioned the index. It stays the most exposed of the broad ETFs to the AI-derating debate.
notes.SMH: Semiconductor ETF at $668.91, up 1.4 percent and a relative winner as memory names like Micron and SanDisk rallied about 5 percent. Chips are the rotation magnet within tech, but the group stays volatile.
notes.SPY: S&P 500 ETF at $744.39, down just 0.3 percent as broad breadth and a defensive rotation offset the mega-cap losses. The core long-term holding for diversified exposure.
notes.AAPL: Apple at $297.01, only fractionally lower and relatively resilient. Memory-cost-driven price hikes and the July 30 report are the near-term watch points.
notes.AMZN: Amazon at $232.79, down 4.7 percent on AI-capex anxiety across the hyperscalers. Retail plus AWS cash generation supports the long-term case; July 30 earnings are the next catalyst.
notes.META: Meta at $563.85, down 2.3 percent in the capex-driven tech pullback. The ad engine and AI roadmap are intact; spending discipline is the market's focus.
notes.GOOGL: Alphabet at $349.68, the worst mega-cap on the day at about minus 5 percent after two senior AI researchers left for Anthropic and OpenAI, plus legal and regulatory headlines. The search, cloud and AI franchise is intact and now cheaper, but the talent and regulatory overhang is real.
notes.TSLA: Tesla at $405.05, up 1.1 percent and bucking the mega-cap selloff. A high-volatility name driven by delivery trends, autonomy news flow and risk appetite; July 22 earnings ahead.
notes.NFLX: Netflix at $72.88, down 5.8 percent and roughly 40 percent off its highs after sitting out the media-merger wave; value screens now flag it as cheap. The ad tier and engagement are structural positives, but momentum is weak.
notes.DIS: Disney at $102.45, down 1.4 percent with the tape. Parks and streaming profitability are the recovery levers; it is less tied to the AI-spend debate than tech.
notes.BRKB: Berkshire at $488.69, essentially flat and acting as the portfolio's ballast. Record cash and a defensive mix make it the steady anchor as volatility and rate uncertainty rise.
notes.DIA: Dow ETF at $517.08, up 0.3 percent and a defensive-rotation winner on a Caterpillar-led blue-chip bid. It is less exposed to the AI-derating than QQQ.
notes.OIL: USO at $112.69, down 1.9 percent and well off the early-June highs above $140 as Iran de-escalation eases supply fears. The underweight stance is intact, though the war-driven inflation flare still feeds Fed hawkishness.
notes.BTC: Bitcoin near $63,950, up about 1 percent and steadying in the low-60Ks while risk assets wobble. A high-volatility holding to size with care.
notes.GOLD: Gold near $4,182, down fractionally and off its recent highs around $4,380 as a hawkish Fed lifts real-yield pressure. Still the portfolio's core macro hedge into geopolitical and rate uncertainty.
notes.SILVER: Silver near $65.5, soft alongside gold and off its highs near $70. Its industrial-demand leg and higher volatility make it the more aggressive precious-metals hedge.
